• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Epiclim Liver Lipidomic - F0 adult females

Baptiste Ducarme

2025-10-14

1 Introduction

This script was used to perform liver lipid profiling in adult female zebrafish (15.5–16.5 months post-fertilization) following developmental exposure (2 hpf to 28 dpf) to combined climate stressors, including acidification (pH 6.35) and elevated temperature (32°C), as well as PFOS exposure (nominal concentration: 2 µg/L), using a 2 × 2 × 2 factorial design.

The lipidomic data, consisting of m/z, retention time features and their associated intensities, were first preprocessed in MZmine, including peak detection, alignment, normalization to internal standards, and filtering based on the %RSD of pooled quality control samples. The resulting dataset, used for all downstream analyses, contained both identified and unidentified features across all samples.

The data were first filtered to retain only features present in at least three samples per condition. To address the few missing values, K-nearest neighbor (KNN) imputation was applied, followed by log2 transformation and Pareto scaling to stabilize variance and approximate normality.

Principal component analysis (PCA) was then performed on the transformed data to visualize the overall sample distribution. Differential analysis was conducted using the limma framework, with experimental condition and exposure included as model factors. Contrasts of interest were defined for each condition versus the control, and MA plots were generated as diagnostic plots. In addition to determining whether each combination treatment elicited a response beyond that predicted by the corresponding individual stressors, pairwise comparisons were performed between each combination treatment and its respective single-stressor treatments.

From the limma output for the condition-versus-control contrasts, the m/z, retention time, t-statistic, and raw p-value were extracted for subsequent pathway analysis. Pathway enrichment analysis was conducted using Mummichog (version 2) in positive-ionization mode, with a mass tolerance of 5 ppm and enforcement of primary ions. The top 10% of peaks ranked by p-value were selected as the significance cutoff. The Danio rerio (dre_mtf) pathway library was used, including only pathway sets containing at least three features. To estimate the null distribution, 100 random permutations were performed. Statistically significant metabolic pathway alterations were defined as those with a Gamma p-value < 0.05 and Hits sig ≥ 3.

2 Load required packages

library("readxl") # open data
library("dplyr") # code in pipe
library("ggplot2") # for graph
library("rstatix") # Analyse stat
library("gridExtra") # For graph alignment
library("ggpubr")# for stat link with ggplot
library("lubridate") #for timpoint analysis
library("multcomp")# stat and letter
library('AER')# stat and letter
library("stringr")
library("ggExtra") # PCA
library("tidyverse") # includes ggplot2, for data visualisation. dplyr, for data manipulation.
library("RColorBrewer") # for a colourful plot
library("ggrepel") # for nice annotations

R.version

3 Working environment setup

setwd("/Users/baptisteducarme/Desktop/EpiCLim-F0-liver-female/Lipids")
rm(list = ls()) # clean the global environment, good coding practice

4 Import dataset

counts <- read_excel("/Users/baptisteducarme/Desktop/EpiCLim-F0-liver-female/Lipids/EPICLIM_F0_Liver_Lipid_Females.xlsx",sheet = "NormData")

4.1 Data treatment

count<-as.matrix(counts)
head(counts) 
## # A tibble: 6 × 42
##   Compound       Lv_r002 Lv_r003 Lv_r004 Lv_r006 Lv_r007 Lv_r008 Lv_r009 Lv_r010
##   <chr>            <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>

## 2 CE fragment: …   NA     1608.  1380.   1596.   1822.     29.5   942.   1491.  
## 3 Cer(d18:1/16:…    1.14   109.    53.8    61.6    23.3     3.12   18.8    82.9 
## 4 Cer(d18:1/18:…    4.96    44.4   24.1    27.7    25.6     6.38    9.75   24.2 
## 5 Cer(d18:1/18:…   NA       15.6    1.07    1.00    4.03   NA      NA       4.27
## 6 Cer(d18:1/22:…   NA      430.   240.    237.     52.3    16.5    43.4   196.

# Convert to matrix
counts_matrix <- as.matrix(counts) 

# Lipid matrix with the first column as name
Lipid_names <- counts[[1]] 
counts_matrix <- as.matrix(counts[,-1]) 
counts_matrix <- matrix(as.numeric(counts_matrix), nrow = nrow(counts_matrix))
rownames(counts_matrix) <- Lipid_names
colnames(counts_matrix) <- colnames(counts)[-1] 
head(counts_matrix,1)
